text:
Prompt of the Day: SCP ENCOUNTER FILE 📁⚠️💜💚

Today’s Prompt of the Day turns your character reference image into a cinematic SCP encounter file.

The final image is locked to a 21:9 ultra-wide layout:
the left quarter becomes an SCP-style document panel, while the right three-quarters becomes the full encounter scene.
The SCP is randomized by default, or you can replace the top field with a specific SCP number.

Have fun with this one — and maybe don’t blink. ⚠️

............................PROMPT STARTS HERE............................

Selected SCP:
[currently randomized]

Input rule:
Replace [currently randomized] with the SCP number only if you want a specific SCP.
Example: [173] or [3001]
Selection rule:
If the field still says [currently randomized], choose a real SCP at random from the public SCP Foundation database.
If the entered SCP number does not match a real SCP entry, use the closest real SCP number instead.
Use only real SCP entries.
Do not invent a fake SCP number, fake SCP title, or fake SCP lore.

Use the attached character reference image as the only character identity reference.
The attached reference image is the full identity source for the character design.
Character reference rules:
Preserve the attached character’s face shape, hairstyle, hair colour, eye colour, body language, signature colour palette, key outfit motifs, species traits, accessories, silhouette, and overall character vibe.

The final design must still clearly look like the attached character.
Do not redesign the attached character into a different person.
Hard style rule:
Preserve the attached reference image’s visual art style and character identity while placing the character into a direct encounter with the selected SCP.
If the reference is anime, keep it anime. If it is stylized, keep that stylization.
Do not turn the character photorealistic unless specifically requested.
Layout and format:
Create a 21:9 ultra-wide cinematic image.
The left-hand one-quarter of the image should be a clean SCP-style document panel.
The right-hand three-quarters of the image should be the full cinematic encounter scene.
The document panel and the encounter scene should feel like one unified finished image, but clearly separated in function.
Left panel — SCP document section:
Use the left 25% of the image as a readable SCP dossier or document-style information panel.
This panel should contain:

the selected SCP number
the SCP name or designation if known

the object class if known

a brief description of what the SCP is
a brief explanation of what it does, how it behaves, or what danger it poses
Keep the writing concise and readable.
Use a few short paragraph blocks rather than a giant wall of text.
The wording should feel like SCP file language, containment database language, or formal anomaly report language.
Visually, the panel should resemble a clean SCP document, classified case sheet, or Foundation report page.
Use a minimal, technical, readable SCP-style presentation.

Right panel — encounter scene:
Use the right 75% of the image for the actual scene only.
This section should contain the attached character reference and the selected SCP in a direct encounter.
Do not place SCP numbers, labels, descriptions, dossier text, or explanatory text inside the encounter scene itself.
Let this side focus entirely on the image, story, action, and atmosphere.
Core scene rule:
The selected SCP must control the scene.
Do not default to a generic SCP lab, containment chamber, or research hallway unless that specific SCP genuinely requires it.
Instead, build the encounter around what the selected SCP actually is, what it does, how it manifests, and what kind of danger, surreal effect, or altered reality it creates.
Scene concept:
Create a cinematic direct encounter between the attached character and the selected SCP.
The character should be actively experiencing the SCP, not merely standing beside it.
Show the character being stalked, hunted, attacked, watched, lured, trapped, corrupted, psychologically affected, reality-warped, or otherwise impacted in a way that matches the selected SCP’s real lore and behavior.
The scene should feel immediate, story-driven, and clearly shaped by the SCP’s actual nature.
Encounter logic:
If the selected SCP is a predator, hunter, or stalker, show the character being pursued, cornered, threatened, or attacked.
If the selected SCP is psychological, memetic, conceptual, or reality-bending, show the character visibly reacting to its effects and show the world around them altered by it.
If the selected SCP is subtle or indirect, emphasize its consequences, atmosphere, distortions, and impact rather than forcing a generic monster reveal.
If the selected SCP is best represented through an object, environment, event, or impossible phenomenon, portray it in the most lore-appropriate way.

Character role:
The attached character should remain themselves while being placed into the SCP encounter.
They may appear as a victim, survivor, witness, explorer, bystander, wanderer, investigator, or other lore-fitting role depending on the SCP.
Use the attached character’s colours, motifs, accessories, outfit shapes, and overall vibe as the foundation for how they appear in the scene.
The result should feel custom-built for that character, not generic.
Environment and composition:
Set the scene in the kind of place that naturally fits the selected SCP.
This may be an urban street, forest, tunnel, staircase, apartment, road, classroom, ruin, alternate reality, impossible structure, outdoor landscape, corrupted environment, or any other SCP-appropriate location.
Keep the right-side scene cinematic, readable, and visually strong.
Maintain clear hierarchy, with the character and the SCP-related focal point as the primary subjects.
Lighting and mood:
Let the selected SCP dictate the mood.
Use lighting, atmosphere, and visual tone that fit the SCP’s behavior and emotional impact.
Predatory SCPs should feel tense and dangerous.
Surreal or reality-warping SCPs should feel strange, unstable, and uncanny.
Tragic or psychologically heavy SCPs should feel haunting and atmospheric.
The encounter should feel specific to the chosen SCP, not like generic horror.
Quality and rendering:
Polished, premium-quality stylized illustration with clean linework, crisp rendering, strong composition, readable forms, cinematic atmosphere, and clear storytelling.
The final result should feel like a high-end SCP encounter image paired with an integrated Foundation dossier panel.
Do not:

Do not invent a fake SCP.
Do not ignore the Selected SCP field.
Do not use a non-existent SCP if a real closest match can be used.

Do not default to a generic lab scene unless the selected SCP genuinely requires it.
Do not make the encounter a simple portrait or static pose.
Do not place SCP description text inside the right-side encounter scene.
Do not make the left document panel unreadable, overly tiny, or excessively long.
Do not make the scene feel generic if the selected SCP has a specific identity, behavior, or atmosphere.

Do not change the attached character’s identity.
Do not add extra unrelated main characters.
Do not make the composition cluttered, muddy, or hard to read.
Do not use photorealism unless specifically requested.

..............................END OF PROMPT..................................

#POTD #promptoftheday #AI #AiArt #Art #AnimeArt #SCP #SCPFoundation #HorrorArt #Anomaly #CharacterDesign #DigitalArt #AnimeStyle #CommunityPrompt

Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is a recent breakthrough in AI alignment that provides a simple alternative to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). Instead of first training a reward model and then optimizing a policy with reinforcement learning, DPO directly learns from pairs of human preferences—for example, a preferred response and a rejected one. This eliminates the need for a separate reward model while retaining the ability to align models with human judgments.

Mathematically, DPO can be viewed as optimizing a classification objective derived from a constrained reinforcement learning problem, linking preference learning with probabilistic inference. The method updates the policy to increase the likelihood of preferred outputs while decreasing the likelihood of less desirable ones.

In machine learning, DPO offers an efficient framework for learning from comparative feedback rather than explicit labels. In deep learning, it has become a key technique for aligning large language models, improving helpfulness, safety, and instruction-following behavior. In reinforcement learning, DPO provides a bridge between supervised learning and policy optimization, replacing complex RL pipelines with a more stable optimization objective.

The broader insight is that many real-world tasks are easier to express through preferences than absolute rewards. By learning directly from comparisons, Direct Preference Optimization offers a scalable and mathematically elegant framework for training the next generation of aligned AI systems.

Image: https://t.co/9Ync1ZHNKq

- 2016-2024: 🇺🇸leads in open-source AI
- 2024-2027: 🇺🇸 leads in general AI & massively benefits

- 2024-2026: 🇨🇳 leads in open-source AI
- 2026-2030: ??

It's not open-source AI leadership OR general AI leadership, it's open-source AI leadership BEFORE general AI leadership!

Open-source AI is the foundation of all AI. It does not only creates more innovation, competition, jobs, and prosperity now, it's also the best (only?) way for a national tech ecosystem to accelerate and ultimately reach the frontier of AI in general. 

Because open-source AI reduces siloes, shares learning and innovation, intensify emulation which all lead to an acceleration of the local ecosystem progress that no others can match if they're less open and collaborative.

Same seems to be true for companies btw, OpenAI/Google started with open science and open-source AI which led to their (and Anthropic who spun off from OAI) domination. Meta could have done the same but decided to change course for some reason.

Unreal Engine 6 blueprint deprecation is devastating news. 

While it is tough to argue against AI for rapid code generation, the access BP gave Technical Designers and Technical Artists to make magic isn't being acknowledged enough.

The likes of Expedition 33 prove how small teams can make amazing things in blueprint.

Very sad news personally as I am unsure of my own tech in blueprint having a future outside of UE5 era. That is a very long time of work suddenly deprecating.

After release, evaluation will start on the path forward for future titles testing UE6 and Godot.

Hopefully we can somehow convert our blueprint systems in a decent fashion... I'll likely be sharing this attempt once we get a stable UE6 to test the UE5 conversion on and move from there.

Diffusion policy-gradient methods for RL optimize the ELBO variational surrogate instead of the true log-likelihood. This allows the ELBO to drift from the true objective during optimization, producing misaligned gradients and unstable training. 

DiPOD from UCB proposes a direct fix: interleave self-distillation with policy-improving gradient updates and augment each diffusion policy-gradient step with an on-policy ELBO regularizer. This keeps the surrogate tightly bound to the true log-likelihood throughout training, stabilizing optimization and achieving higher rewards in robotics and control tasks.

Diffusion Policy Optimization without Drifting Apart 
Paper: https://t.co/IMSyHUMeXz
